VNet + NSG + Private Endpoint pattern: MFIs show virtual networks, network security groups, private endpoints and private DNS co-occurring with web apps and certificates (support 0.0409), so plan the finance web app and Azure SQL backend inside the same VNet protected by NSGs and use Private Endpoints with Private DNS to keep all traffic internal.

Key Vault hardening conventions: tenant Key Vaults universally enable purge protection and mostly retain soft-deleted objects for 90 days (enablepurgeprotection True 100%, softdeleteretentionindays 90 = 94.4%), therefore design the finance app to store secrets in Key Vault with purge protection and soft-delete enabled and additionally restrict Key Vault network access (move from broad public access toward firewall/perimeter or VNet rules).

User-assigned managed identities are standard: many resource types use user-assigned identities (storage accounts userAssigned 55%, databases identity UserAssigned 100%) and MFIs frequently include managedidentity, so plan to attach a user-assigned managed identity to the web app and grant it least-privilege access to SQL and Key Vault rather than embedding credentials.

Monitoring and logging are expected: Application Insights and Log Analytics/OperationalInsights commonly appear alongside app services in MFIs, so include an Application Insights component and a Log Analytics workspace for the finance app and forward database telemetry and alerts to the same workspace for unified observability and alerting.

Storage redundancy and network posture: Storage accounts predominantly use Standard_LRS (48.5%) with a meaningful share using geo‑replicated SKUs and mixed publicnetworkaccess (Enabled 53.8% / Disabled 44.3%) and networkacls defaultaction Allow (92%), therefore choose storage redundancy (GRS/RAGRS) only if cross-region DR is required and explicitly disable public network access and tighten network ACLs for finance data.

App Service sizing and exposure conventions: web sites and serverfarms frequently use Basic and modest PremiumV3 SKUs (B1 28.8%, P0v3 16.9%) and site public network access is enabled tenant-wide, so when planning the internal finance web app pick an App Service Plan sized for expected load but configure private access (VNet integration or Private Endpoint) and consider upgrading SKU if SLA/compute needs justify it.

Azure SQL deployment pattern: existing databases frequently use vCore Hyperscale SKUs (Hyperscale 60%, HS_Gen5 common) and are provisioned with user-assigned identities and no zone redundancy, so evaluate Hyperscale versus GeneralPurpose for the finance backend based on size and I/O, provision identity-based authentication, and explicitly review zone/geo‑replication settings to meet RTO/RPO requirements.

Use of perimeter WAF devices for ingress: Application Gateways with WAF_v2 and public IPs appear in MFIs alongside web apps and NSGs, indicating use of managed WAFs for ingress, so if any external access is needed consider placing the app behind an internal or external Application Gateway with WAF (or Front Door) depending on exposure needs, otherwise avoid public ingress for a finance app.

Private connectivity for platform services: MFIs link Key Vault, storage, SQL and other platform services with VNets and private connectivity patterns and a non-trivial number of private endpoints exist tenant-wide, therefore plan to place Azure SQL and Key Vault behind Private Endpoints and populate Private DNS zones for seamless internal resolution as part of the finance app network design.
